speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 28, 1996. The survivor I'm interviewing is Frieda Fela Stern, S-T-E-R-N, S-Z-T-E-R-N, whose maiden name is Rojzenman, R-O-J-Z-E-N-M-A-N. My name is Evita Smordin. We're in the city of Winnipeg, province of Manitoba, country of Canada. And this interview will be conducted in English. [INAUDIBLE]. My name is Evita Smordin, E-V-I-T-A S-M-O-R-D-I-N. Today is October 28, 1996. The survivor I'm interviewing is Fela Stern, F-E-L-L-A S-T-E-R-N. We are in the city of Winnipeg, province of Manitoba, country of Canada. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fela Stern.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-E-L-A S-T-E-R-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you spell your name when you lived in Poland, Stern?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-- well, I wasn't married then. So I didn't have that name when I lived in Poland

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was single.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rojzenman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-J-Z-E-N-M-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: A city called Wlodzimierz. Volynsk, That's the province.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you born in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born March the 18th, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me something about your parents? What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Dov Rojzenman. They call him Beryl in Yiddish. My mother's name was Bella Rojzenman. Her-- her name-- her maiden name was Frost.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a bubbe and zayda living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I had two bubbes and two zaydas. One of them died before the war in '38. She died of cancer. My zayda was still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that your father's parents or your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents. My mother's parents were-- my zayda was in Warsaw coming home. They killed him on the border, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] apparently, according to people who knew. And they humiliated him. He had a beard, and he was accosted. They were cutting his beard and-- and throwing things at him before they killed him. But-- we were no witness to that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1940. My bubbe died with my mother and my siblings in first ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one sister, who was three years old. And my little brother was born in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were younger than you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was just a couple of months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all did not survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tziril, [INAUDIBLE]. She was named after my father's mother who was in Jewish, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your little brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And going back to when you were a child, you lived in the same town as your parent-- grandparents. Did you spend time at your grandparents' house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. A lot of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your grandmother's house, grandfather, your mother's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a-- a two-room apartment they were renting. And it had a big porch. [SIGHS] My grandparents from my father's side had a four-plex, which was four entrances into the house. Each-- they were renting three of them. And they lived in one. Each one had a separate entrance from south, west, north, and east. And there was a yard. I remember a tree, a wood shack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also Hasidim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's parents were Hasidim. Were your father's parents Hasidim?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were religious Jews. I don't know if they were Hasidim. [BACKROUND NOISE] I-- I don't really--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your mother's parents were Hasidim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sect of Hasidism?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called [INAUDIBLE] rabbi. I-- I think that's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually was wearing [INAUDIBLE] all my life from the [INAUDIBLE] rabbi. My zayda from my mother's side was with the rabbi most of-- of his-- of the year. He just came home twice a year. And he was with the rabbi all the time. My grandmother was the one that was running the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of store was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a leather store. They were selling leather for making boots, complete cuts, pre-cut. He never did anything. He was just with the rabbi. He was coming home for Passover. He was coming home for Sukkot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was very happy to bring up the family and to work, make a living for them all. And when he was going to the rabbi, she still sent money there. I don't think we were rich. We were just comfortable, never knew any hunger or anything like that. I went to a Hebrew school called Tarbut. I started in [NON-ENGLISH], which is preschool. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember my school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a large building with a big yard, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] classrooms. I remember my [NON-ENGLISH] which is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Principal.

speaker: interviewee
text: The principal. His name was Olin. I don't remember the name of my teachers, because they changed every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a teacher for each subject. There was a teacher for Tanakh. And there was a teacher for Hebrew. And there was a teacher for history. Each subject had a different teacher. I liked school very much. I don't think I had a favorite teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a close friend when you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had two girlfriends. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One of them, I spent a lot of time with. I can't remember her first name, but her second name was Kaufman. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] She also had no siblings. I had no siblings at that time. And she had no siblings. So we spent after-school time together. [SIGHS] And the rest of the class was close. We-- we had a rule that we should speak Hebrew not only in school but outside of school. So there was-- we never knew who was-- the policing that they-- to watch us speak Hebrew outside of school. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was-- We used to go start school at 8 o'clock in the morning. And then have 11 o'clock, 15 minutes intermission. And then I think we went home about 1:30 or 2 o'clock-- I'm not sure-- for the rest of the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would have been about 15, 20 minutes walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I walked alone. There was nobody on my street that went to that school. I had to go through to public schools. There was no problem during the week. But we were the only school that was open on Sunday. And when I walked with my books on Sunday, everybody knew who I was. And I was bothered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes they would throw rocks. And sometimes they would just try and catch the books. And sometimes they would-- I had pigtails. They would just drag my pigtails and call me names most of the time from far away. But after a while, you get so used to it that you just ignore it and don't even look their way, pretending that they're not there. But inside, you're always scared. It was something we lived with for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it was like to have Shabbos in your house when you were little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos, I remember going with my grandparents to shul. I spent most of the time outside of the shul just playing with the other kids but going in once in a while just to-- to check on them. Although I don't remember praying much. But it's funny how much you observe when you're a child, because after the war, when I had a chance to go into a shul, I could open any prayer book and know exactly where the rabbi or the chazzan was praying. Although I've never done it as a child, it remained with me. And I sort of subconsciously remembered all the prayers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What preparations were made for Shabbos on Friday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They start at 4 o'clock in the morning, baking and then cooking. So by 8 or 9 o'clock, she was ready for business and everything was prepared. There were favorite things. There was always a ritual, sort of. Challah was baked. There was some apple cholent baked. There was onion buns for-- for Friday. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I knew what I was eating every day, because every day had a menu. Monday-- on Friday was Shabbat dinner. And Saturday was the continuation of-- of Shabbat. And in the evening, after Havdalah, there would be just sort of different things. And on Sunday, we would have hamburgers. And then Monday, we would have something else. There was always-- the-- the-- the meals were prepared according to the days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have fond memories of the synagogue. I remember a lot of people from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I didn't know I remembered them. But when I met people after the war, I-- very little. My school had three grades of five. When I went to grade five, there was Olin [? Bet, ?] and [? Gimmel ?] And each-- each room from grade five had between 27 and 30 children. And only two of us were alive after the war. She lived in Paris. She married a local boy. And she lived in Paris. Her name was Yehudit Goldstein. And me. We were the only two out of the whole school. I don't know how many children went to the other grades. But I only know how many children were in my grade. And there is one young girl who survived. She was a year younger than us. And she-- she went to our school. Her brother was in our grade. She survived because she was shot and pretended not to be alive. And she was lying among the dead till a-- a-- a guy, a farmer came, because the earth was moving. And he came to look at it. And he saw when she was trying to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he crossed himself. And he said, if God left you alive, I'm gonna help you. And he took her into his house, washed her off all that blood. And she was only shot in her foot. He took care of her. And she's in Israel now. I don't know her married name. But she's alive, and she's married. I know that her name was Weinstein when she went to school. So she's another one who survived from a younger generation.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's two more boys who survived, older than us. They were in finishing school. I can't remember their names. But I met them after the war. One of them actually married this Goldstein girl who was with me in my class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you used to do with your girlfriend after school. What did you play when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: We you didn't do all of the things children do here. The first thing we were supposed to do is homework. And her parents had a sugar factory. So all the candies were wrapped, hand wrapped. And we used to sneak into the factory and try to learn how to hand wrap those candies and taste a lot of them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then we would go home and spend time with the family. Most playing was going on in the schoolyards.

speaker: interviewer
text: What games did you play? Do you remember any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We played ball games. I'm not sure how you-- two kids would throw the ball and one in the middle would try to avoid it. And if you were hit by the ball, you changed with the one that-- that hit you. Something like [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] volleyball.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any toys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a doll and a-- a carriage for the doll. I had a tea set. And that's all the toys I had, no other toys that I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had an auntie who made some clothes for my doll. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you got the doll?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about seven. [SIGHS] Then-- when-- when I had vaccination, and I had the infection in my arm, somebody bought me a little guitar, was like a toy guitar. So that was another toy I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the doll's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't have a name. Was just-- just a doll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a pet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. During the war, I had a big dog. My mother was afraid that I'm going-- Jewish people didn't really engage in having dogs or anything. But my mother was afraid I'm gonna grow up to be selfish, because I didn't have any siblings till later in my life. So she got me a dog so I would share things with him and learn to share. The dog's name was Lucy. It was a German Shepherd. And we didn't know how clever that dog was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knew that it was a she, that she was clever, because when it was 11 o'clock, she would come to school and spend the 15 minutes with me at recess and then go back home. But during the war, the Germans took her away. And she ran away from them and came home. And then when she would smell or hear or-- or sense that the Germans were coming, she would run up to the top floor on-- on a ladder, which is very unusual for a dog to do, and a big dog at that. But she knew that she had to hide. So we found out how clever she was during the war. But then they-- they took her away again. I don't know if-- of they kept her or shot her or whatever they did with her. But we didn't have her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change for you when you were younger? How did you know that something was going different, that the war was starting?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first tragedy in our family was when the first bomb fell, the first German bomb. This was when they start the war with Russia. We were divided. Our city became Russian in '39. And just a few kilometers away was the river that divided the Russian border with the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what river was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bug. B-O-G. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember it was early in the morning. And what I thought was thunder was the beginning of the war. And the first bomb fell not far from our house. And it hit-- hit one of our cousins. And this is when-- because families were so close, we found out immediately that he was in the hospital, and he was hurt. And he was the only son of my mother's first cousin. So this was the first time that I realized everybody was crying and everybody was upset about it. And then things start creeping in. More people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't going to Tarbut anymore. That school was changed to Russian school. And the teacher who was teaching us Tanakh became the teacher who was teaching us Russian literature, Communist ideas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they changed the curriculum.

speaker: interviewee
text: They changed completely. It wasn't a private school anymore. Some of the teachers stayed. And some were different teachers, because the requirements were different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it still a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not anymore. Anybody could've gone to that school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I start being sick at that time. And I-- I missed a lot of school. They didn't really know what was wrong with me. But I had terrible pains. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later in my life, I found out that it was gallbladder then already. And then I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] scarlet fever. And that was-- that was a funny time in my life that I'll never forget, because I thought it was a punishment. I was going to school. And then after school, I was supposed to do my homework. But I went to visit a girlfriend instead. And we played. And I didn't do my homework.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next day, when I had to go to school, I was terribly afraid because they checked the homework every single day. And I knew the teacher will see that I hadn't done my homework. I would get a note on my thing saying-- so I figured since I'm being so over-protected by everybody, I'll just say I have a sore throat and skip school that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I told that to-- to my parents, they immediately asked my grandmother to look into my throat. She took a spoon, looked into my throat, and told my mother that I have a sore throat, and we should go and see the doctor. That scared me, because I knew I was lying about the sore throat. But they took me to the doctor anyway. And when we came there, he said, it's a good thing you came. She has scarlet fever. And I was sure that I wasn't sick. It was just the punishment for telling a lie. And I knew that God would punish me. So I was punished with the scarlet fever for telling a lie about school. I never forgot that, not because I was so sick, which I was. I was in quarantine. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] can't remember exactly. I was in the hospital quite a while. And I remember anything they brought in to the hospital, they couldn't take it home again. So-- so I-- I-- I missed quite a bit of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just a teenager. I mean, I was 12, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
